headline: McDonald's focus on value and a big new burger drive sales in the first quarter
summary: McDonald’s posted better-than-expected sales in the first quarter as a new burger — and a continuing emphasis on value — brought in customers.  McDonald's shares rose more than 3% before the opening bell Thursday.  The limited-time Big Arch burger —- a 1,020-calorie behemoth that went on sale in the U.S. last month — became a viral sensation after McDonald’s CEO Chris Kempczinski posted a video of himself taking a nibble from one.

headline: Why McDonald's may be a better consumer story than an investment play
summary: McDonald’s (MCD) topped Q1 estimates after four straight quarters of same-store sales growth, but CEO Chris Kempczinski warns business could slow next quarter. Yahoo Finance’s Julie Hyman and Head of News Myles Udland break down why McDonald’s may be a stronger consumer story than an investor story.

content.title: Why McDonald's may be a better consumer story than an investment play
content.description:
McDonald’s (MCD) topped Q1 estimates after four straight quarters of same-store sales growth, but CEO Chris Kempczinski warns business could slow next quarter.

Yahoo Finance’s Julie Hyman and Head of News Myles Udland break down why McDonald’s may be a stronger consumer story than an investor story.
content.summary: McDonald’s (MCD) topped Q1 estimates after four straight quarters of same-store sales growth, but CEO Chris Kempczinski warns business could slow next quarter. Yahoo Finance’s Julie Hyman and Head of News Myles Udland break down why McDonald’s may be a stronger consumer story than an investor story.

headline: McDonald's misses US sales growth target as consumer spending tightens
summary:
STORY: McDonald's missed its U.S. sales growth target in the first quarter.

The world's biggest burger chain posted U.S. same‑store sales growth of 3.9% - below Wall Street estimates.

Thursday's update showed low-price meal deals and limited-time offers struggled to draw diners.

Consumers have recently pulled back on spending due to higher fuel and grocery costs.

CEO Chris Kempczinski said the operating environment was still "challenging."

That was depsite McDonald's beating quarterly estimates for revenue and profit.

Several U.S. restaurant chains like Wingstop and Domino's have reported weaker quarterly sales growth.

They cited a hit to customer spending from soaring gasoline prices caused by the Iran war.

Wall Street analysts also argue lower-income consumers have turned more selective.

It's seen buyers trade down to simpler, single‑item orders rather than full meals.

Data from Placer.ai showed U.S. traffic was uneven at McDonald's through the first quarter.

Same-store visits fell 1.3% in January due to winter storms.

Traffic bounced back just under 4% in February on pent‑up demand, but slipped again in March.

On a global scale, McDonald's found comparable sales rose 3.8% - slightly behind analyst forecasts.

Although it was an improvement on last year's numbers.

The firm's total revenue of $6.52 billion beat estimates.

content.title: McDonald's misses US sales growth target as consumer spending tightens
content.summary:
STORY: McDonald's missed its U.S. sales growth target in the first quarter.

The world's biggest burger chain posted U.S. same‑store sales growth of 3.9% - below Wall Street estimates.

Thursday's update showed low-price meal deals and limited-time offers struggled to draw diners.

Consumers have recently pulled back on spending due to higher fuel and grocery costs.

CEO Chris Kempczinski said the operating environment was still "challenging."

That was depsite McDonald's beating quarterly estimates for revenue and profit.

Several U.S. restaurant chains like Wingstop and Domino's have reported weaker quarterly sales growth.

They cited a hit to customer spending from soaring gasoline prices caused by the Iran war.

Wall Street analysts also argue lower-income consumers have turned more selective.

It's seen buyers trade down to simpler, single‑item orders rather than full meals.

Data from Placer.ai showed U.S. traffic was uneven at McDonald's through the first quarter.

Same-store visits fell 1.3% in January due to winter storms.

Traffic bounced back just under 4% in February on pent‑up demand, but slipped again in March.

On a global scale, McDonald's found comparable sales rose 3.8% - slightly behind analyst forecasts.

Although it was an improvement on last year's numbers.

The firm's total revenue of $6.52 billion beat estimates.

headline: BK Pres. on burger wars: 'We're outperforming the industry right now'
summary: The burger wars are heating up. Burger King (QSR) is taking a slightly different approach, focusing on consumer input and evaluating the brand staples rather than launching new products. Tom Curtis, Burger King President US & Canada, joins Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi to discuss what’s new with the Whopper, the operational improvements driving the brand’s turnaround, and why he believes Burger King is outperforming rivals—including McDonald’s (MCD), which is signaling a potential slowdown next quarter.

headline: Shake Shack stumbles as fast‑food demand softens; shares plunge
summary:
STORY: Shares of Shake Shack plunged as much as 30% on Thursday and were heading for their worst day ever after the restaurant chain reported a quarterly profit loss and missed Wall Street's revenue estimates.

Shake Shack said it was hurt by rising commodity costs, including beef, and weak consumer spending.

And it's not the only fast‑food chain seeing consumers tighten their belts.

McDonald's, Domino's and Papa John's all reported weaker quarterly sales growth, signaling pressure on consumer spending from rising gas prices driven by the U.S. war in Iran.

Companies like Chipotle and Restaurant Brands International have also flagged rising beef prices, which have set records due to dwindling U.S. cattle supplies.

Shake Shack executives said on a post-earnings call that the company's short-term results will continue to be impacted by the ongoing war in the Middle East.

content.title: Shake Shack stumbles as fast‑food demand softens; shares plunge
content.summary:
STORY: Shares of Shake Shack plunged as much as 30% on Thursday and were heading for their worst day ever after the restaurant chain reported a quarterly profit loss and missed Wall Street's revenue estimates.

Shake Shack said it was hurt by rising commodity costs, including beef, and weak consumer spending.

And it's not the only fast‑food chain seeing consumers tighten their belts.

McDonald's, Domino's and Papa John's all reported weaker quarterly sales growth, signaling pressure on consumer spending from rising gas prices driven by the U.S. war in Iran.

Companies like Chipotle and Restaurant Brands International have also flagged rising beef prices, which have set records due to dwindling U.S. cattle supplies.

Shake Shack executives said on a post-earnings call that the company's short-term results will continue to be impacted by the ongoing war in the Middle East.

headline: Recent earnings signal strain on low-income consumers, expert says
summary:
STORY: McDonald's was among the companies on Thursday that said higher gas prices linked to the Iran war were disproportionately hurting low‑income consumers, as the burger chain warned of a weak start to its second quarter.

"The lower end seems to be really struggling at this point," Buchanan said. "And this earnings season really has highlighted that. From Whirlpool to McDonald's, we've seen some of these names and corporations really communicate [that] in a very direct way."

"The tipping point is an inflection in labor," Buchanan continued. "If we have a labor blip where jobs aren't as plentiful... we really feel like there's a lot of stress that could emanate throughout our marketplace" that could pressure U.S. stock prices.

content.title: Recent earnings signal strain on low-income consumers, expert says
content.summary:
STORY: McDonald's was among the companies on Thursday that said higher gas prices linked to the Iran war were disproportionately hurting low‑income consumers, as the burger chain warned of a weak start to its second quarter.

"The lower end seems to be really struggling at this point," Buchanan said. "And this earnings season really has highlighted that. From Whirlpool to McDonald's, we've seen some of these names and corporations really communicate [that] in a very direct way."

"The tipping point is an inflection in labor," Buchanan continued. "If we have a labor blip where jobs aren't as plentiful... we really feel like there's a lot of stress that could emanate throughout our marketplace" that could pressure U.S. stock prices.

headline: If You Want to Know How Much Inflation Has Really Hurt U.S. Consumers, Skip the CPI and Study Big Mac Burger Prices
summary: This analysis explores the gap between official core CPI and real-world costs using the Big Mac as a constant. Hedonic adjustments create a statistical illusion of growth while masking a long-term decline in purchasing power.

headline: MoneyMasters Podcast 5-7-26- As 60-40 Investing Fails, Expect a "Great Migration"
summary: In this episode, Larry McDonald joins the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast to discuss what he calls the "Great Migration" of capital from tech and growth stocks into hard assets. The founder of The Bear Traps Report explains why the traditional 60/40 portfolio is failing – and why investors should consider a significant allocation to commodities like gold, silver, base metals, and energy.
